name: Galleon Ship Design
description: A large, multi-decked sailing ship used as both a cargo carrier and a warship. Its design integrated a lower forecastle and a square-rigged profile, making it a stable gun platform and a capable ocean-going vessel.

dependencyEdges.note: Broadside naval artillery shaped the galleon's lower, more stable warship design with gun ports and heavy cannon batteries, while the hull type also served merchant cargo roles.

sources.title: Galleon
sources.source_locator: Evolution of Mediterranean Ship Design and The Galleon Design sections say the galleon was created for naval warfare after boarding was replaced by heavy cannon fire, had lower forecastles, gun ports along both sides, and heavy cannons below decks.

dependencyEdges.note: Caravel-era Iberian oceanic sailing designs and mixed-rig seafaring capability were shipbuilding predecessors to broadside-capable sailing warships; this edge does not claim caravels themselves carried broadside batteries.

sources.title: Caravel

name: Printing Press

The press mechanism and shop equipment relied on woodworking, but it is an enabling craft dependency rather than the core invention.

name: The Reformation

name: Scientific Method

region: Europe, especially Renaissance and early modern natural philosophy

dependencyEdges.note: Scientific method grew out of natural philosophy and debates over explanation during the Scientific Revolution.

Alchemy contributed laboratory and experimental traditions that preceded early modern chemistry and method, without being a strict requirement.

name: Sovereignty Theory (Bodin)
description: The political concept, articulated by Jean Bodin, that the state possesses absolute and perpetual power within its territory. This theory provided a philosophical foundation for the modern, centralized nation-state.
region: France / French Wars of Religion and early modern sovereignty debates

dependencyEdges.note: Machiavelli's prince-as-supreme-within-territory argument is a direct early-modern conceptual predecessor to Bodin's explicit sovereignty doctrine.

sources.title: Sovereignty
sources.source_locator: Early modern political philosophers section: Machiavelli is described as giving the prince supreme authority within his territory, while Bodin is identified as the first European philosopher to treat sovereignty extensively.

dependencyEdges.note: Bodin's sovereignty formulation grew from legal-humanist and civil-law scholarship, making formal legal-system traditions contextual infrastructure rather than an ancient law-code prerequisite.

sources.title: Jean Bodin
sources.source_locator: Life and Methodus sections: IEP says Bodin studied and taught law at Toulouse, worked as an advocate at the Parlement of Paris, and combined historical and legal study with legal-humanist methods.

dependencyEdges.note: Reformation-era confessional conflict and the stripping of Catholic temporal power formed the political context for Bodin's sovereignty doctrine, but did not supply a procedural method.

sources.title: Sovereignty

sources.title: Jean Bodin

scopeNote: Covers Bodin's 1576 sovereignty doctrine in Les Six livres de la Republique: supreme territorial authority as the source of human law. It excludes generic state formation, ancient law codes, Westphalia, Hobbes, Rousseau, and later constitutional-sovereignty debates.

name: Triangular Trade
description: A complex transatlantic trade network connecting Europe, Africa, and the Americas. It involved shipping manufactured goods to Africa, enslaved people to the Americas (the Middle Passage), and plantation crops back to Europe.

sources.source_locator: Britannica states that enslaved Africans were taken to plantations and that plantation crops and products, including sugar, rice, indigo, tobacco, cotton, molasses, and rum, were shipped to Europe as the third leg of the triangular trade.

sources.publisher: Nevada Bureau of Mines and Geology
sources.source_locator: Introduction, History, and Fire Assaying Today sections: assayers determine metal content in rock for mining decisions; Agricola described fire assay in 1556; modern practice mixes a known amount of carefully sampled powdered rock with fluxes and weighs/parts the bead.

sources.title: De Re Metallica
sources.publisher: Project Gutenberg / Herbert and Lou Henry Hoover translation
sources.source_locator: Preface and Book VII opening: the preface identifies the seventh book as assaying ore; Book VII says tests determine whether ore contains metal, whether much or little, how to separate it, and whether smelting will compensate expenditure.

sources.title: De Re Metallica, Translated from the First Latin Edition of 1556
sources.publisher: Missouri University of Science and Technology Scholars' Mine
sources.source_locator: Catalog record: lists the Hoover translation of Agricola's 1556 De Re Metallica and includes Book VII plus comments identifying mining methods, metallurgical processes, geology, mineralogy, and mining law through the sixteenth century.

scopeNote: Covers small-sample fire assays used for ore valuation and treatment decisions in mining. It excludes full-scale blast-furnace iron production, generic smelting capacity, and modern automated analytical laboratories.

name: Newspaper Advertising
description: Paid notices in periodicals for goods, shipping departures, auctions, medicines, and services.

dependencyEdges.note: The scoped practice is paid notices inserted into a recurring newspaper; the Boston News-Letter solicited advertisements in its first weekly issue and carried the first paid ads two issues later.

sources.title: Advertising in America
sources.source_locator: MHS says the Boston News-Letter began as the first regularly published newspaper in British North America, solicited small notices at a reasonable rate in its first issue, and carried the first three paid advertisements in issue number three dated 1-8 May 1704.

sources.title: The Boston Newsletter, number 1
sources.source_locator: Collections Online says number 1 was dated 17-24 April 1704, appeared weekly until 1776, and concluded with Campbell's notice that the News-Letter would continue weekly while soliciting advertisements and subscriptions.

sources.title: Advertising in America
sources.source_locator: MHS says the first paid advertisement in an American newspaper appeared in Boston, after the Boston News-Letter began publication on 17 April 1704 and first carried paid advertisements in issue number three dated 1-8 May 1704.

sources.title: The Boston Newsletter, number 1
sources.source_locator: Collections Online identifies number 1 as 17-24 April 1704, describes the Boston News-Letter as the first continuously published newspaper in America, and says Campbell's final notice solicited advertisements and subscriptions.

scopeNote: Covers paid notices inserted into recurring newspapers, anchored by the Boston News-Letter's 1704 advertisement solicitation and first paid notices. It excludes broader handbill advertising, accounting back offices, advertising agencies, and later classified-ad platforms unless represented separately.
